headline: The Humanoid Leaves the Demo Floor
deck: UBTech calls its new UWORLD U1 the first full-size humanoid built for mass production and logged 13,361 orders on launch day; its industrial Walker S2 is already shipping. Bank of America expects the fleet to climb from thousands now to millions this decade. What limits that ramp sits upstream, in memory and power.

art.caption: Every humanoid on a production line is a standing order for the memory and power that feed it.

body: China's UBTech used a 30 June launch to move humanoids from the stage to the assembly line. It described the new UWORLD U1 as "the first in the world designed for mass production," offered it in three trims from 119,800 renminbi, and said cumulative orders had reached 13,361 units on launch day [E1]. Its industrial predecessor, the Walker S2, has already entered mass production and begun deliveries, with orders topping 800 million yuan [E1].

The scale ambition sits above any single robot. Bank of America projects roughly 90,000 humanoid sales in 2026 as makers stand up their first commercial fleets, annual shipments rising from about 20,000 units in 2025 to 10 million by 2035, and three billion humanoids in operation by 2060 [E2]. Those are forecasts, not deliveries, but they set the direction the capital is pointed [E2].

The constraint has moved upstream. A humanoid is a rolling demand for inference chips, high-bandwidth memory and, behind them, electricity, and that is where the market is placing its biggest bets. SK Hynix, which holds 57 percent of global high-bandwidth-memory revenue, is pricing the largest-ever foreign listing on Nasdaq on exactly that thesis [E3]. The robot is no longer the hard part; feeding it is.

Read carefully, the launch is a supply-chain claim as much as a robotics one. UBTech is asserting it can build at volume, and BofA is asserting the buyers will be there, and both assertions terminate at the same bottleneck of memory and power that the AI-capex cycle already strains [E1][E2][E3]. If the humanoid decade arrives, it arrives on the back of the compute build-out, not beside it.

The nulls are worth keeping in view. Launch-day order tallies are marketing until units ship, and 13,361 orders booked is not 13,361 robots delivered [E1]. Multi-decade population forecasts compound small assumptions into large numbers and should be read as scenarios, not schedules [E2]. The discriminator is boring and decisive, audited shipment counts and installed fleets, not press-release orders, and those are the figures the desk will track next [E1][E2].

dissent.argument: Order books convert to revenue slowly and forecasts to 2060 are close to unfalsifiable. The durable signal is Walker S2 deliveries and memory pricing, not a launch-day tally or a three-billion-unit headline.

evidence_box.fragment: UWORLD U1 launched 30 June as "the first in the world designed for mass production"; 13,361 cumulative orders on launch day; Walker S2 in mass production with orders over 800 million yuan
BofA: ~90,000 humanoid sales in 2026; shipments from 20,000 in 2025 to 10 million by 2035; three billion units by 2060
SK Hynix holds 57% of global high-bandwidth memory revenue; launching ~$28 billion Nasdaq ADR listing on AI-memory demand
